old_claim.note: Shamanism & Animism provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Mythic narratives such as Gilgamesh and other ancient epics circulated through oral-traditional systems before surviving as written texts.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica describes oral tradition as the first mode for transmitting knowledge, art, and ideas and says Gilgamesh was a broadly distributed tale in the ancient Middle East before being inscribed on tablets.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Oral tradition carries long-form epics and mythic tales before their written survival.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The source supports oral narrative transmission as the predecessor for surviving mythic texts, while the older shamanism edge was a speculative religious-practice overlap rather than a documented dependency.

ontology_before: Mythology & Cosmology depended on Shamanism & Animism, implying prehistoric shamanic or animistic practice directly enabled the node.
ontology_after: Early Written Mythic Cosmologies is scoped to Mesopotamian written mythic texts with oral-traditional narrative background.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: mythology_and_cosmology now points to oral_tradition_storytelling and no longer directly depends on shamanism_animism.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped back to prehistoric shamanic practice rather than written mythic cosmologies.
A source shows the earliest scoped Mesopotamian mythic texts did not draw on oral-traditional narrative systems.
The shamanism_animism node is split and a narrower animistic-worldview node becomes the source-backed prerequisite.
short_reason: Oral tradition is the source-backed narrative predecessor; shamanism was too speculative for this scoped node.
